primary_concept: Integrating an LLM API to generate dynamic, personalized language learning content
secondary_concepts: Free vs. paid API tradeoffs and cost awareness
API key security and safe storage
Persisting user vocabulary progress with localStorage or a JSON file
Combining persistence and external APIs in a single app
Scoping an LLM-powered feature to avoid runaway costs
mvp_features: User selects a target language from a fixed list (e.g., Spanish, French, Japanese)
App calls an LLM API to fetch 5 vocabulary words with translations and example sentences for the chosen language
Each word is displayed as a card showing the word, translation, and one example sentence
User can mark a word as learned, and that status persists across page refreshes using localStorage
A simple progress indicator shows how many words have been marked as learned
stretch_features: Daily word rotation: a new set of 5 words is served each day, with previous days' words accessible in a history view
User can type in a custom word or phrase and ask the LLM for its translation, pronunciation tip, and two example sentences
Difficulty filter: user can request beginner, intermediate, or advanced vocabulary
Quiz mode: hide the translation and let the user guess before revealing the answer
forbidden_features: User authentication or login systems — these require a database and back-end session management beyond the scope of this course
Payment processing or premium tier gating — explicitly identified in the course as a signal to bring in a developer
Multi-user data sharing or leaderboards — requires a full database and identity management
Building a custom translation model or fine-tuning — far beyond agentic-coding scope
Storing API keys in client-side code without environment variable protection — violates the API key security lesson
Automatic or scheduled server-side jobs (e.g., cron jobs to push daily words) — requires server infrastructure not covered in the course
learner_outcome: Learners will be able to build and deploy the Language Learning App, demonstrating that they can wire an LLM API into a functional web app, protect their API key, and persist user progress — combining every skill from all three modules into a single shipped project.
instructor_talking_points: Show the PRD and phased plan before touching the coding agent — reinforce that upfront planning from Module 1 prevents scope creep even on an LLM-powered app
When adding the LLM API call, narrate the difference between using an LLM to build the app versus using an LLM as a live feature inside the app — a distinction introduced in Video 3.4
Demonstrate setting a spending limit or rate guard on the API key before running the app, making the cost and security lessons from Video 3.2 concrete and visible
After Phase 1 works, pause and save to version control before adding the daily word rotation stretch feature — reinforcing the save-before-big-changes habit from Module 1 Video 4
scope_warnings: Adding a login screen so each user has their own word list: this requires a full database and user authentication system — jump to this only after the course and with a developer
Asking the LLM to generate audio pronunciation for every word: text-to-speech APIs introduce a second paid API dependency and a new cost surface; save for a post-course enhancement

Building a spaced-repetition algorithm (like Anki): implementing SR scheduling logic is complex functional work that will overwhelm both the learner and the coding agent in a single session — keep progress tracking to a simple learned/not-learned toggle for Phase 1

Fetching words from a third-party dictionary API in addition to the LLM: chaining two external API calls in the same phase multiplies failure points and debugging complexity; introduce only in Phase 3 if at all

Deploying with a visible API key in the front-end code: tempting shortcut that contradicts the security lesson — the instructor must explicitly show environment variable or server-side proxy setup before deploying
